lead: Automated Testing with JUnit, Mockito + Claude Code
contents: The Problem: “I Tested It Manually” What Is JUnit? Test Anatomy: Arrange, Act, Assert Testing Edge Cases: Where Bugs Actually Live Running Tests with Maven Test Suites as Eval Datasets What Is Mockito? AI-Augmented: Claude Code Generates Tests Tests That Test Nothing TDD with Claude Code Offline Evals and Online Evals Exercise 4.1: Write Tests by Hand Exercise 4.2: AI-Generated Tests — Critical Review Exercise 4.3: TDD with AI Test Coverage Module 4: Key Takeaways

body.alt: 50 Shades of Green - It's almost green, just some broken tests

items: You tested the happy path once
You forgot edge cases
You won’t test again after every change
When the code breaks in 3 months, nobody knows how to test it

body.quote: Automated tests run every time, test everything, and never forget.

title: What Is JUnit?
desc: The standard testing framework for Java (current: JUnit 5 Jupiter)

body.code: import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*; class CalculatorTest { @Test void addTwoPositiveNumbers() { Calculator calc = new Calculator(); assertEquals(5.0, calc.add(2.0, 3.0)); } }
body.note: @Test = this method is a test. assertEquals = verify expected matches actual.

title: JUnit Lifecycle: Setup and Teardown
desc: Five annotations control what runs before, during, and after tests.

body.code: class OrderServiceTest { @BeforeAll // once before ALL tests (static) static void startDatabase() { ... } @BeforeEach // before EACH test method void createTestOrder() { ... } @Test void cancelledOrderRefundsPayment() { ... } @AfterEach // after EACH test method void cleanupTestData() { ... } @AfterAll // once after ALL tests (static) static void stopDatabase() { ... } }

items: @BeforeAll → (@BeforeEach → @Test → @AfterEach) ×3 → @AfterAll
@BeforeAll / @AfterAll are static — shared expensive setup (DB, server)
@BeforeEach / @AfterEach — fresh state per test, prevents test coupling

title: Test Anatomy: Given, When, Then
desc: Every good test has three parts

body.quote: Given / When / Then. Every test. No exceptions.

items: Cucumber + Gherkin — .feature files in plain English, backed by Java step definitions
Karate — API testing with built-in assertions, no Java glue code needed

title: Testing Edge Cases: Where Bugs Actually Live

body.note: Edge cases: zero, negative numbers, very large values, null, empty strings — these are the inputs that break code.

body.code: mvn test # Passing: [INFO] Tests run: 5, Failures: 0, Errors: 0 [INFO] BUILD SUCCESS # Failing: [ERROR] CalculatorTest.divideTenByTwo: expected: <5.0> but was: <4.999> [INFO] BUILD FAILURE

body.quote: The build fails. You cannot package or deploy with failing tests.

title: Test Suites as Eval Datasets
desc: Your test suite follows the same structure used to evaluate AI systems.

rows: Multiple components working together (DB, API, services)
Basic sanity — does the app start at all?
table.note: Same purpose: verify that the system behaves correctly before releasing it. In this course we focus on unit tests and integration tests.

title: What Is Mockito?
desc: Your class depends on a database or API. How to test without real infrastructure?

body.note: A mock pretends to be the real dependency. You verify the right methods were called without real infrastructure.

title: Mockito: Stubbing with when/thenReturn
desc: Mocks can also return fake data — so you control what the dependency does.

items: when(...).thenReturn(...) — stubbing: control what the mock returns
verify(...).method(...) — verification: check the mock was called correctly
when(...).thenThrow(...) — simulate errors (DB down, timeout, 404)

body.code: You: Generate JUnit tests for Calculator (add, divide, modulo). Claude Code generates 8 tests: @Test void addPositives() { assertEquals(5.0, calc.add(2, 3)); } // ✅ Good @Test void addNegatives() { assertEquals(-5.0, calc.add(-2, -3)); } // ✅ Good @Test void divideByZero() { assertThrows(ArithmeticException.class, () -> calc.divide(1, 0)); } // ✅ Good @Test void divideNormal() { assertEquals(2.5, calc.divide(5, 2)); } // ✅ Good @Test void moduloWorks() { assertEquals(1, calc.modulo(10, 3)); } // ✅ Good @Test void testLargeNumbers() { calc.add(999999, 999999); } // ❌ No assertion! @Test void testCalculator() { assertNotNull(calc); } // ❌ Tests Java, not you @Test void moduloNegative() { assertEquals(-1, calc.modulo(-10, 3)); } // ❓ Right answer?
body.note: 5 good, 2 worthless, 1 questionable. The moduloNegative test assumes Java’s % operator behavior — is -1 correct or should it be 2? AI didn’t think about this. You must.

body.card: Your review checklist for AI-generated tests:
items: Does every @Test have an assertEquals or assertThrows?
Does the assertion test your code, not Java itself?
Are edge cases covered? (zero, negative, MAX_VALUE, null)
Would the test fail if you introduced a bug?
Is the expected value actually correct? (AI can assert wrong answers confidently)

body.code: // BAD — no assertion @Test void testAdd() { Calculator calc = new Calculator(); calc.add(2, 3); // runs but proves nothing } // BAD — tests Java, not your code @Test void testMath() { assertEquals(5, 2 + 3); // yes, Java can add... } // BAD — always passes regardless of code @Test void testDivide() { assertNotNull(new Calculator()); // proves Calculator exists }

body.quote: AI can generate all of these. You must recognize them.

title: TDD with Claude Code
desc: Test-Driven Development: write tests FIRST, then implementation

body.code: You: I want to add a sqrt() method to Calculator. Write the tests first. Don’t implement the method yet.

body.note: Then: implement sqrt() to make these tests pass.

title: Offline Evals and Online Evals
desc: Your testing strategy mirrors how production AI systems ensure quality.

body.code:
Code change → Offline evals → Regression gates → Production
→ Online evals + traces → Failure analysis → (back to code)
body.note: You’re already doing this with JUnit + Git + Maven.

items: Write 5 JUnit tests for multiply() by hand (no Claude Code)
Cover: positive, negative, zero, large numbers, decimal precision
Run mvn test — do they pass?
Introduce a bug in multiply() — does a test catch it?

body.quote: Goal: Understand testing before asking AI to generate tests.

items: Ask Claude Code: “Generate comprehensive tests for my Calculator”
Read every test critically
Find at least one test that is weak or tests nothing useful
Find at least one edge case Claude Code missed
Write that missing test yourself
Run mvn test
You want to add factorial(int n) to Calculator
Ask Claude Code to write tests first (no implementation!)
Read the tests — do they cover: 0, 1, positive, negative (should throw)?
Implement factorial() to make all tests pass
mvn test — all green?

body.quote: Rule: Implementation comes AFTER tests. This is TDD.

body.code: # Add JaCoCo plugin, then: mvn test jacoco:report # Open target/site/jacoco/index.html

items: Line coverage: which lines executed during tests
Branch coverage: which if/else branches tested
80%+ is a typical professional target

body.quote: Coverage reveals blind spots. But 100% coverage with meaningless assertions is worse than 70% with strong edge case tests.

items: Automated tests catch regressions that manual testing misses
Every test: Arrange, Act, Assert
Edge cases catch real bugs (zero, null, negative, overflow)
Mockito creates fake dependencies for isolated testing
Claude Code generates tests — but watch for false confidence
TDD with AI: let AI write tests first, you implement
Industry: test suites = eval datasets; test gating = regression gates
Always read AI-generated tests: “What does this actually prove?”
